---
title: "Power analysis for "
author: 
  - name: Sichao Wang
    degrees: PhD
    email: wangsic1@msu.edu
    affiliations: 
      - name: Michigan State University, Center for Statistical Training and Consulting
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%Y-%m-%d, %H:%M:%S %Z')`"
title-block-banner: "#01665e"
format: 
  html: 
    toc: true 
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left 
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 4
    code-fold: false 
    code-tools: false 
    embed-resources: true 
    theme: default 
css: idastyle.css
execute: 
  eval: true 
  echo: false #fenced
  output: true 
  message: false 
  warning: false 
  error: false 
  include: true 
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    out.width: "150%"
bibliography: references.bib
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

# Project Overview:

Title: 

Project PI's:

<p>

-   
-   

</p>


<h3>Primary Objective:</h3>

<p>**The objective of this study is to -.**</p>

<h3>Secondary Objective:</h3>

<p>

</p>



<h3>Key references:</h3>

There are a few key references for this report: @, @ and @.



::: {.callout-note title="Project Updates" collapse="false"}
-   For power analysis and sample size determination

:::
```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false

pacman::p_load(## file folder setup
               fs,   # creat folder dir_create()
               here, # easy to do cross reference in the project.
               ## Data step
               readr,
               readxl, # read_excel()
               tidyverse,
               janitor,
               ## IDA
               Hmisc,
               naniar, ## for missing data 
               VIM, ## for missing data 
               ## Model
               meta,
               metafor,
               dmetar, #for find.outliers()
               
               lme4,
               glmmTMB,
               splines, #for bs() term
               survival,
               coxme,
               riskRegression,
               performance,
               logistf,
               DHARMa, #use simulatResiduals()
               broom,
               emmeans,

               #Imputation
               mice,

              #Bayesian
               brm,



               ## result reporting
               ### graph
               ggplot2,
               ### table
               gt,
               gtsummary,
               knitr,        # for kable()
               sjPlot,      # tab_model()
               gtExtras, # for get table theme like gt_theme_538()
               patchwork,

              #inverse probability weighting
               adjustedCurves,
               WeightIt,
               cobalt,
               pammtools,

               MASS

               )


set.seed(42004063)

###################### color scales

gg_color_hue <- function(n) {
  hues = seq(15, 375, length = n + 1)
  hcl(h = hues, l = 65, c = 100)[1:n]
}

colshue = gg_color_hue(10)

#plot(1:10, pch = 16, cex = 2, col = colshue)

library(scales)
hex <- hue_pal()(6)


###### colorblind colors

# colorbrewer
colsbrewer=c("#8c510a", "#d8b365", "#5ab4ac", "#01665e")

# Okabe-Ito
cols10<-c("#E69F00","#0072B2","#D55E00","#009E73","#F5C710","#56B4E9","#CC79A7","#999999","#000000","#01665e")



#https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/colorBlindness/vignettes/colorBlindness.html

```



```{r}
#| label: import-data
#| include: false

## Use gender as an example first.

Dat_gender <- read_excel(here("Data/Raw/.xlsx"), 
    sheet = "", skip = 2) >| 
  clean_names() >| 
  rename(study="x1",male_BE="m_2",female_BE="f_3",male_EAC="m_4",female_EAC="f_5",male_CTR="m_6",female_CTR="f_7") >|
  mutate(year=str_extract(study, "\\d+"),author=str_extract(study, "[A-Za-z-]+"))


#saveRDS(df_behavioral_CD , file = here("Data/Clean/Dat_behavioral_CD.rds"))
```



# Power analysis 
## 
```{r}
#| label: import-data
#| include: false
# Install necessary packages if not already present
# install.packages("PowerTOST")

library(PowerTOST)

### 1. Define Parameters
alpha <- 0.05       # Significance level
target_power <- 0.80 # Desired power (80%)
icc <- 0.5          # Intra-class correlation
sd_pop <- 25        # Estimated population SD from previous studies (e.g., 20-30 mg/dL)

# In a crossover with ICC=0.5, CV (Coefficient of Variation) is often used for power.
# Let's assume a mean triglyceride peak of 100 mg/dL for CV calculation.
typical_mean <- 100 
cv_estimate <- (sd_pop / typical_mean)

### 2. Define Equivalence Margin (Theta)
# This is the "no impact" zone. 
# 0.80 to 1.25 is the standard for bioequivalence (20% margin).
lower_margin <- 0.80
upper_margin <- 1.25

### 3. Calculate Sample Size for 2x2 Crossover
# This accounts for the crossover design and the within-subject variability.
sample_size <- sampleN.TOST(alpha = alpha, 
                             targetpower = target_power, 
                             logscale = TRUE, 
                             theta1 = lower_margin, 
                             theta2 = upper_margin, 
                             CV = cv_estimate, 
                             design = "2x2")

print(sample_size)

### 4. Adjusting for Covariates (DFC and Fasted Triglycerides)
# Adding covariates generally reduces the 'residual error'.
# Rule of thumb: If your covariates explain R-squared = 20% of the variance,
# your effective CV decreases, potentially reducing the N required.

# Function to estimate N reduction if covariates are effective
adjust_for_covariates <- function(n_original, r_squared) {
  return(ceiling(n_original * (1 - r_squared)))
}

# Assuming DFC and Fasted levels explain 30% of the variance:
n_adjusted <- adjust_for_covariates(sample_size$`Sample size`, 0.30)
cat("Suggested N after adjusting for covariate precision:", n_adjusted, "\n")
```
